I think that the current version of the code is fine. The timers callbacks don't ever call `ResetTimer()` anymore, so don't have to worry about `mTimer` being mutated by the callbacks. They may change other timer values on the same TCP endpoint, but a timer callback in one TCP endpoint won't ever affect timers on another TCP endpoint. So we can just iterate over the linked list once. For each endpoint, we iterate over its timers twice: once to fire any callbacks, and a second time to determine the earliest timer.

Well, ideally I think it should be `Dict[str, ResourceMixin]` or `Dict[str, ResourceBody]`, but none of them declares the required class-level field `resource_type`. It is just dynamically declared on all subclasses of `ResourceBody` that are actually using the decorator `Directory.register`.

Adding it to the mixin would require in fact to declare a method that allows you to retrieve the resource type, but we will need to use some advanced mypy techniques like `Protocol` to make it happy.

We could also add the class-level property to `ResourceBody`, but I fear that it will not play nice with the parent class `JSONObjectWithFields`, or could have undesirable side-effects for classes subclassing `ResourceBody` but do not need the `resource-type` property.

At the minimum we can declare `Dict[str, Type[Any]]`, it is not great, but at least we know that this is a dict mapping strings keys to class definitions. I did that.

To be host, the `service_name` is something we desperately tried to avoid in these "generalized" parsers.
Since there is already an `S3RequestParser`, we could check this case and modifying the `uri_params` and afterwards calling the super method. Something like this:
```python
class S3RequestParser(RestXMLRequestParser):
    def _parse_shape(
        self, request: HttpRequest, shape: Shape, node: Any, uri_params: Mapping[str, Any] = None
    ) -> Any:
        if shape is not None and uri_params is not None \
                and shape.serialization.get("location") == "uri" \
                and shape.serialization.get("name") == "Key" \
                and request.url.split("?")[0].endswith(f"{uri_params['Key']}/"):
            uri_params = dict(uri_params)
            uri_params["Key"] = uri_params["Key"] + "/"
        return super()._parse_shape(request, shape, node, uri_params)
```

Another option would be to externalize the location parsing to a separate function, but this doesn't seem to be worth the effort / complexity.

> Until now I guess I have been disregarding data shuffling almost entirely since there's no guarantee in any point of this dask module that certain workers will be dispatched any individual part of the training set

`lightgbm.dask` does prevent shuffling of the data, I think, by explicitly checking which workers hold different parts: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/72d7010b5ae09818541a749bd02b455b5a08de5b/python-package/lightgbm/dask.py#L350-L355.

But now that you mention it I'm actually not sure what the behavior would be if, for example, `data` and `label` had different partitioning. Maybe this would fail or maybe something bad would silently happen :grimacing: 

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/72d7010b5ae09818541a749bd02b455b5a08de5b/python-package/lightgbm/dask.py#L323

Either way, thanks for confirming that I have the right mental model here! **I don't think you should mess with this right now and that it's a good candidate for followup PRs.**

Your proposal makes sense to me as another way to chunk things up, but I don't think it changes the risk of shuffling. I can't think of a reason that `X_e.partitions[0]` and `data.partitions[0]` (for example) are more likely to be on the same worker than they are to be on different workers. Similarly, I can't think of a reason to believe that `X_e.partitions[0]` and `X_e.partitions[1]` would be more likely to be on the same worker than they are to be on different workers.

Yes, the order of the keypoints is super important. Indeed, we train models to predict left-right elements, and their order in the vector indicates if they are left-right.

If we don't carry this information properly, it will be impossible to apply those transformations to the object, or they will be wrong.

And I strongly believe we should take transformations into account when defining what should be in the Feature. This is one of the main reasons why we have Feature to begin with.

I believe we should add enough metadata that geometric transformations are well-defined. `image_size` is an easy case, and we do take this information into account as well in the `(x0, y0, x1, y1)` bounding-box world (we know that 3rd element is x1, and this changes how we perform flip).

"safer" == "greater probability of the module being available in the unpickling environment and of the object being defined in it (and pertain to it)"

For example, consider a module named `pack.mod`. At loading its state with `load_module()`, we can assume that both Standard Library modules and other modules from the package `pack` are available (otherwise, `pack.mod` itself wouldn't be loadable). So, if at saving its state with `dump_module()`, the unpickleable object `pack.mod.obj` can be saved globally either as `std.lib.obj` or as `pack.other.obj`, should we prefer one over the other? I argue that yes, because the most likely scenario is that the object is defined in the Standard Library module `std.lib` and imported from it in both `pack.mod` and `pack.other`. Plus, there's a small chance that, in the unpickling environment, the module `pack.mod` may exist but `pack.other` may not (for example, if we had slightly different versions of the package). For StdLib packages, however, that's highly unlikely.

That's the logic behind the whole hierarchy. It's an empirical way of maximizing the probability of success of saving by reference in the module's namespace.

This is almost orthogonal to the stratification of self/"builtin"/"user" behavior from that table. The only change is that modules that are part of the same package as the module being saved (if the module is part of any package) are put together with the "builtin" modules even if the package is not appropriately installed. Basically, this hierarchy will define which reference to another module is better, but not if that should be used instead of a reference to the module itself, or if an exception should be raised.

Unless you have any brilliant ideas on how to get log output capturing to work, I think the path forward is probably to disable or delete all the test cases that are currently failing.  That would still leave us with good coverage of most of the config parameters, and certainly the standard *paths*, but there would be a couple of config parameters that would be un-tested.  Code paths that might be of concern (esp. if we delete the log-based tests entirely) would be:
* `logLevel` is hard to test reliably if you can't capture logs
* I did find a case or two where `presolve` had an impact on the actual solving, but it feels a little brittle to me to rely on that (e.g. a future upgrade to the solver might change that), so I didn't write the test that way.  `presolve` is relevant because it's passed as an argument to the solve function rather than applied as an option to the model, i.e. it's a different code path that wouldn't be tested other ways.

If you're okay with tests that *hit* all the code paths, but don't necessarily provide hard evidence of them having been hit (i.e. we hit them to make sure they don't crash, but we don't necessarily verify that they had an impact on the solving), that's a fairly trivial adjustment to make.

It was due to a hidden flaw in this kind of unit tests in fact. Normally, the reverter module will write the revert command of a command executed in the relevant storage file. So this an array of strings, basically. However, since `mock_run_scripts` is mocked here, what you get from it as a revert command is not an array of strings, it is a MagicMock  instance.

With the implementation where you just write this down directly in a file, it does not raise an exception, because you can always get a string representation of every object, even if its the default one (something like `"<MagicMock id='2373950194576'>"`).

However, when the storage was a json file, then it would throw an exception because MagicMock instances are not serializable, on the contrary to basic types (str, int) and basic containers (dict, list).

All of this to said that with the pure text representation, it is not a problem anymore, and I remove this code. But it wanted to write down somewhere that theses kind of unit tests will have funny behaviors if you try to invoke any logic about fallback in them.

You should check the file format of the file under `image_path`. This line simply assumes target that internal flash starts at 0x08000000 *and* the image format is `.bin`. The `.bin` format is quite problematic as it doesn't include the loading address information - if the `.bin` format is preferred, then the elf->bin converter should somehow provide the loading address to be used here.

Another issue with `.bin` format is that it doesn't really support disjoint flash sections. For example, if user provides his own linker file which includes the QSPI section on STM32F746 target, then the resulting bin file is over 2 gigabytes (MCU flash startings at 0x08000000 while the QSPI starts at 0x90000000).

The much better alternative in such situations is `.hex`. See https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbed-os/issues/9789#issuecomment-466314199 for example.

If the image format is `.hex`, you should use `jlink_script.write("loadfile {}\n".format(image_path))` instead. `.hex` format includes all the addresses, so there's no need for `build_jlink_script()` to know them.

If the `.hex` file contains QSPI flash, it might be necessary to enable flashing external memories *before* calling `loadfile`/`loadbin`. You can do so using `exec EnableEraseAllFlashBanks`.

Below is fully working jlink script I use to program 32F746GDISCOVERY board (onboard ST-Link upgraded to JLink using https://www.segger.com/products/debug-probes/j-link/models/other-j-links/st-link-on-board/) with the `.hex` file that contains both internal flash and QSPI flash data.
```
device STM32F746NG
speed auto
si 1
exec EnableEraseAllFlashBanks
r
st
loadfile target.hex
r
g
qc
```

Note that the `r` and `st` commands after `exec EnableEraseAllFlashBanks` are needed. If you call `loadfile` directly after `exec EnableEraseAllFlashBanks`, then `SEGGER J-Link Commander V6.42b (Compiled Feb  5 2019 17:33:07)` fails to flash the QSPI memory on 32F746GDISCOVERY discovery board. I didn't find any mention of that in the JLink documentation, I discovered it by trial and error.

Running tests on installed copies is very easy:

```
pip install pyregion[test]
pytest --pyargs pyregion
```

And is the recommend way.  I even updated the docs to do this. This ensures test dependencies are installed.

One can also run tests on the installed version if you are in a git repo checkout doing development:

```
pip install .[test]
pytest --pyargs pyregion --import-mode=importlib
```

Which will install the package in site-packages and then run the tests from in there as well, ensuring not to add `.` to sys.path.  This is more convoluted, but it is the way forward for developers using `pytest`.

I handled this case in the `_roll_out_time_series` separately. Because the sort column is an increasing number throughout all `id`s and `kind`s in this case, the index build from the old `id` and the `sort` column does not make much sense. Example:

You fill in data like
```
id  value
1  a
1  b
1  c
2  d
2  e
```
and the added sort column will be
```
id  value sort
1  a        1
1  b        2
1  c        3
2  d        4
2  e         5
```

If I would use that value, we would end up with some ids names `id=2,timeshift=5`, which would not make much sense (in my opinion). Rather, they are now named `id=2,timeshift=0` and `id=2,timeshift=1` .

Can we unexpectedly remove other multicast addresses (e.x. `All Network Backbone Routers Multicast Address`) here, even though the chance is low. 
In the current implementation, `mAllDomainBackboneRouters` will contain the network prefix of last domain prefix when the device had BBR service enabled. When BBR service is turned off, `mAllDomainBackboneRouters` never change. Whenever this code runs, it will try to remove `mAllDomainBackboneRouters` which could equal to other subscribed multicast address. 
For example, if the current mesh local prefix equals to the old domain prefix set in `mAllDomainBackboneRouters` (a totally valid prefix setting), this code will unsubscribe `All Network Backbone Routers Multicast Address`.

I suggest a simple fix that we always set up `mAllDomainBackboneRouters` as long as `Get<BackboneRouter::Leader>().HasDomainPrefix()`.

I'll describe the use case to give you a proper example how I think of it.

You have a mapping hierarchy like so:

- records
  - article
  - dataset
  - video

*(For simplification no prefixing is configured)*

```python
# Let's assume that you only want to create the `records-video` index:
current_search.create(index_list=['records-video'])

# This will now only go through the creation operations for the `records-video` 
# index, and thus add it to the `new_indices` dictionary with its respective 
# prefixed + suffixed index name.
assert new_indices == {'records-video': 'records-video-1234567'}  # timestamp prefix

# When you reach the point where you have to create the `records` alias,:
alias_indices = self._get_indices(tree_or_filename)
# "self._get_indices" doesn't take "index_list" into consideration
assert alias_indices == ['records-article', 'records-dataset', 'records-video']

# Then you basically want to figure out the final index names 
# (with prefix/suffix) in order to be able to properly update the alias
alias_indices = [new_indices.get(i) for i in alias_indices]
assert alias_indices == [None, None, 'records-video-1234567']

# Now if you just check if `None` is in the "alias_indices" list, you will
# bail out on adding the newly created index to the "records" alias

# Instead IMHO, the logic should be that you definitely want to create aliases
# for the index you've just created.
```

Yes, double negative normally results in a positive, see below

```python
>>> DT[:, :, sort(-(-f[0]))]
   | C0
-- + --
 0 |  0
 1 |  1
 2 |  2
 3 |  3
 4 |  4

[5 rows x 1 column]
```

Why it should be descending? Minus sign just specifies what value should be used for sorting, in the above case it is sorted by `-f[0]`, and the `reverse` parameter controls the overall sorting direction: whether it should be done asc or desc. So from the user point of view the result of `DT[:, :, sort(-f[0], reverse=True)]` should be asc sorting.

With respect to deprecating the `reverse` parameter, I would say it can be pretty useful in some cases. For instance, sometimes one doesn't know in advance if the sort should be done asc or desc, it may be an input boolean parameter or whatever else, then it is easier and more readable to write something like `DT[:, :, sort(f[0], reverse=MY_INPUT_PARAMETER)` than converting `MY_INPUT_PARAMETER` to +-1 and then multiply it by `f[0]`. 

May be @st-pasha also had some other use-cases in mind when [proposed](https://github.com/h2oai/datatable/issues/792) the API.

You need to create it. Look at how sorting works, for instance. We do not create a new data column with the sorted data, instead, we create a `RowIndex` and fill it with ids of the sorted elements. 

So what you need to do here is to create a new `RowIndex` and 
- for non-missing elements fill it with the same row ids as in the input column;
- for missing elements fill it with the row ids of the corresponding non-missings.

Once you have such a `RowIndex` you will simply apply it to your original column as `col.apply_rowindex(ri)`.

The AutoAugment policies are fixed per dataset and usually people use it as-is. Although one theoretically could reproduce the paper and learn a policy on a new Dataset, this is unlikely to be be economically viable for most users. That's why my implementation does not focus on extensibility (custom policies/Datasets) and puts all these variables hidden in the constructor of the object. 

To address the immediate comment here, the problem is that the number of bins is related to the search algorithm that AutoAugment used. Since on their paper they encode the policy by referring to the index of the magnitude range (see final pages on the paper), I thought it would be a good idea to implement the code exactly as described on paper. Given the above encoding, one can't just change the number of bins without updating the number of indexes. Doing so will also mean they don't use the AutoAugment policy recommended by the paper anymore but something else. Finally the reason why I use a constant here is to indicate that we use 10 bins, unlike some implementations that use 11.

We would have to use `partition()` instead of `rpartition()` to get the right result.  Once we know that the header starts with `"HTTP_"` it seems more clear to me to just remove the prefix directly.

This is the counterexample for why `rpartition()` doesn't work:
```
>>> "HTTP_X_HTTP_METHOD".partition("HTTP_")
('', 'HTTP_', 'X_HTTP_METHOD')
>>> "HTTP_X_HTTP_METHOD".rpartition("HTTP_")
('HTTP_X_', 'HTTP_', 'METHOD')
```
For the special headers like `"CONTENT_TYPE"`, this shows why we can't use `partition()` without checking if the `"HTTP_"` prefix is present:
```
>>> "CONTENT_TYPE".partition("HTTP_")
('CONTENT_TYPE', '', '')
>>> "CONTENT_TYPE".rpartition("HTTP_")
('', '', 'CONTENT_TYPE')
```

> 
> > "A ConjunctiveGraph is an (unnamed) aggregation of all the named graphs in a store.
> > It has a `default` graph, whose name is associated with the graph throughout its life.
> > :meth:`__init__` can take an identifier to use as the name of this default graph or it
> > will assign a BNode."
> 
> `graph_id` is asserted to be the identifier of the ConjunctiveGraph and therefore the identifier of the default graph and, as the quad uses `graph_id` as the context identifier, the statement appears in the default graph, as described in the docstring and the serialization is correct.

Thank you very much for the thorough review, you are indeed correct, if this is the identifier for the default graph, then the serialization is correct, so the behaviour here is as intended. I got confused because I did not expect the identifier here to be associated with the concept of the default graph from RDF abstract syntax as that should not have an IRI, or any graph name at all (not even a BNode) as per:

https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-dataset

> 4. RDF Datasets
>
> An RDF dataset is a collection of [RDF graphs](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-rdf-graph), and comprises:
>
> - Exactly one default graph, being an [RDF graph](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-rdf-graph). The default graph does not have a name and may be empty.
> - Zero or more named graphs. Each named graph is a pair consisting of an [IRI](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-iri) or a blank node (the graph name), and an [RDF graph](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-rdf-graph). Graph names are unique within an RDF dataset.

In defense of the current behaviour however, ConjuctiveGraph is not a Dataset, and it is not possible to assign a default graph ID to a Dataset, even though it has one which is internal to RDFLib:

https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/1cba9d87c84b40252fcd7bed9d566839c77e078a/rdflib/graph.py#L1872

I'm just not sure what the right interface is here. A `TransferData` object actually has a relevant environment because it has a `submission_id`. So, just to write down what I'm thinking, if you are setting the environment at runtime, usage might be like so...
```python
env: str = get_env_from_config_file()

# not looking things up, usages might be a little incorrect
ac = ConfidentialAppAuthClient(
    client_id=creds[env].client_id,
    client_secret=creds[env].client_secret,
    environment=env
)

tc = TransferClient(
    authorizer=ClientCredentialsAuthorizer(ac, TransferClient.scopes.all),
    environment=env
)

tdata = TransferData(tc, ...)
tdata.add_item(...)

timerc = TimerClient(
    authorizer=ClientCredentialsAuthorizer(ac, TimerClient.scopes.timer),
    environment=env
)
hourly_job = TimerJob.from_transfer_data(tdata, utcnow(), 3600, environment=env)

timerc.create_job(hourly_job)
```

Looking at this, I see three things.
1. You need to specify `environment=env` _many times_, and if you make a mistake things will not work in somewhat opaque ways
2. Each time, it's for a client init except for `TimerJob`, so that still stands out to me
3. The interface in this case is already not _great_, so maybe it doesn't matter much to make it slightly worse in the name of simplicity

In that light, this seems not that critical to me to try to get this "perfect" because it will be a little bit awkward no matter what we do. `environment: str | None = None` as a new parameter seems like the easiest solution.

As of current CPython master:

* `PyUnicode_AsUTF8String` is simply a call to the internal `_PyUnicode_AsUTF8String` with a `NULL` error handler.
* `PyUnicode_AsEncodedString` runs `PyUnicode_Check`, `unicode_check_encoding_errors` (which does nothing in a normal release Python version without `-X dev`), and then compares the encoding string to `utf8` and variants. On match, it calls `_PyUnicode_AsUTF8String`. This was the shortest code path I found for UTF-8 encoding with an error handler.

There is a faster path in `PyUnicode_AsEncodedString` when `encoding` is `NULL`, which avoids the string comparison. However, this isn't explicitly documented. The method's description just says that

> *encoding* and *errors* have the same meaning as the parameters of the same name in the Unicode [encode()](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.encode) method.

`str.encode`, of course, defaults to UTF-8. So `NULL` is practically the way to use the default `encoding` value, but I couldn't find any confirmation for that in the docs, so I'm hesitant to use it. This might be something to bring up upstream. If confirmed as intended/guaranteed behaviour, that'd basically cut it down to an unnecessary `PyUnicode_Check`.

Reading this logic I think in some (rare) corner cases, we may get to bad state. Here is situation I can think of:
- Suppose user has already called `Add` with a bunch of values for a key. Say there are 3 records: `r0(first), r1, r2` (first one is marked as `IsFirst`).
- User now calls `Set` for the same key, it would add a new record at the end with `IsFirst` set -> `r0(first), r1, r2, n0(first)`.
- Now user issues a `Delete` for index 0 (we can have a vulnerability here):
  - We run through the algo: `r0` is marked as deleted, `r1` is marked as first. We get to `n0` and it is marked as deleted.
  - Now the records in the flash are `r1(fist), r2.`
  - So we get some old entries back in the list.

The only way I can think of for us to get into such situation is if user mixes  `Set` and `Add` for the same key. I think we currently don't use this from core, but would be good to be aware of this limitation.

```python
instr.ch(1).long_integration_time = 30
```
or
```python
instr.ch(1).long_integration_time = 'AUTO'
```

where the former would result in this SCPI being issued

```
:SENS1:LINT:TIME 30
```

and the latter involving this SCPI

```
:SENS1:LINT:TIME:AUTO
```

An aspect to consider, though, is that there is a similar `pulse_current_time_auto` for automatically setting pulse current measurement parameters. Where long integration involves a single property, pulse current mode involves three distinct properties (`pulse_current_time_high`, `pulse_current_time_low`, and `pulse_current_time_average`).

So a question would be whether allowing a user to set any one of the three pulse current properties to `'AUTO'`, detecting this case, and  make it issue `:SENS<ch>:PCUR:TIME:AUTO` (thus effectively having it automatically set all three), or alternatively leaving things as-is, with methods for each "automatic" setting, adhering a bit more closely to the underlying SCPI exposed by the instrument.

```python

def iter_validators(validators_string):
    """Iterate over each complete validator."""

    while validators_string:
         # do whatever you need to yield each individual validator

```

I like the principle of going fully pull-based, but I'm missing something about the implementation.  In particular I'm not sure what you had in mind about splitting the stream into three streams.

There are various online discussions about this (e.g. http://paddy3118.blogspot.com/2013/06/filtering-iterator-into-n-parts-lazily.html is particularly relevant), but they all depend on `itertools.tee()` or similar.  The issue is that If the multiplexer tries to pull from stream A to exhaustion first, then the splitting mechanism has no choice but to memorize all of stream B along the way.  Of course the multiplexer could try to alternate among the streams, but that could amount to the same thing, depending on the distribution of the events among the three types, and the order in which they arrive at the splitter--which the multiplexer can't know about.

I wondered whether our multiplexer can be smart based on the event timestamps (i.e., not letting one of the three iterators get too far ahead of the others in time).  However, the batching within each `_*RequestBuilder` obviates that possibility, because a single pull at the multiplexer level would produce many pulls at the splitter level-- which could cause one stream to dramatically outrun the others, despite the multiplexer's best efforts to keep them synchronized.

The upshot is that I think the solution in this PR actually does exactly what we want, which is to emit requests from the multiplexer in the optimal order--i.e., it requires no memorization of the input stream apart from what is needed for batching anyway, precisely because of the push nature of the component request builders.

Of course I'm very happy to discuss further, especially if you have another solution to these concerns!

Not directly related to this PR, but you can improve the signature and typing of the `deprecated` decorator.

First, `self` isn't used aside from being passed as first argument to func. In this case, it's not necessary to split it out from `*args` and thus you also don't need to use `Concatenate`.

Second, `replacement` is typed as `str` but can also be `None`. Better to type it as `Optional[str]`.

Third, `deprecated` itself doesn't have a return type. Thus mypy will assume `Any` and accept everything.

```py
def deprecated(replacement: Optional[str] = None) -> Callable[
    ["Callable[_P, _R]"], "Callable[_P, _R | None]"
]:

    def decorator(func: "Callable[_P, _R]") -> "Callable[_P, _R | None]":
        def _func_wrapper(*args: "_P.args", **kwargs: "_P.kwargs") -> "_R | None":
            ...
            return func(*args, **kwargs)

        return _func_wrapper

    return decorator
```

Each partitioner is equivalent to a subgraph_backend, and each partitioning strategy is equivalent to a subgraph_property. Down in the c_api.cc changes, we create a 1-to-1 correspondence: we create a subgraph backend/property for each one registered in the external library:
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/17034/files#diff-3288207540b433242fe8da1390c68d3fR719-R744

This means that when we go to actually partition the graph, the regular well-tested subgraphing APIs are being used to apply the subgraph backend/properties to actually partition the graph. These are the same ones that Intel using now for their MKLDNN partitioning. 

So I dont think we need an explicit test for this in this PR. This will be better tested when Intel starts considering using these APIs to implement their partitioning. In the meantime this is more of a forward looking feature, and this is just the first step in that direction.

Just to check my understanding of what's at stake here, by listing out some of my assumptions/interpretations (to be corrected if off-base, plz!):
- This seems specifically relevant for the case where there's a system-wide installation of the service with a shared cross-user KOLIBRI_HOME, which is currently only the case for the GNOME app.
- The concern @dylanmccall is raising is that if the frontend app (which runs in user-space) is able to choose which user to log in as, without some way to prove to the backend that it should have the rights to that user, then someone logged in as user A could (with some poking around with internals, but within the rights of their OS user account) log in and access the data of another user.
- This concern is specifically relevant if we're *not* making everyone a super admin, as noted, since otherwise they can access any user's data anyway once logged in.
- The concern is also only relevant if the OS user accounts can't directly read from the KOLIBRI_HOME directory, since otherwise they could read from the Kolibri DB and access any user's data as well. I imagine the app could be made to function without access to KOLIBRI_HOME except from the background system service, but I'm not sure of whether it currently does that.
- All that being said, if the prior two bullets do hold up, I do understand the concern in principle, and agree that it would be ideal to restrict access for cases like this (i.e. specifically for the way the GNOME app behaves, at least on Endless OS -- I don't think it would currently apply anywhere else?).
- The reason @dylanmccall proposes using D-Bus as a way to get a user-specific token is that it's one way for the app to demonstrate to the Kolibri server process that the token request is coming from a particular actual user, rather than taking it at its word.

Regarding that last bullet, another alternative that comes to mind would be creating and storing a token (non-world-readable) in the user's own data directory, and then associating that token with the user in the backend -- and then the app could re-use that token for re-logging in.[1] For creating a super-admin account, it could use something like visudo to drop its token into a "known admin tokens" directory in KOLIBRI_HOME, thereby proving that the user of that token should be allowed to have an admin account in Kolibri. Not sure whether that's more, or less, general-purpose than relying on D-Bus, but might achieve similar ends.

[1] Update: after reading back through the thread again, I think this is basically where the conversation was heading -- with "the desktop app would get back its own auth token and hold on to that to authenticate with". While the simple version of that approach covers the "can only log in as that same user when you return" case, the piece needs to be covered is distinguishing whether to make the OSUser an admin or learner -- which it seems could be addressed either by D-Bus or the `visudo` type approach I described above.

I'm not convinced that having `DocSpan`s to denote windows over a list of tokens is helpful. I think the code is simpler when you materialize each sliding window: Consider a document with 1000 tokens, `doc = [0] * 1000`. We can get the windows explicitly as `window1 = doc[0:100]; window2 = doc[50:150]` etc. Of course you need to calculate the window-specific start and end positions when creating the window. But this should allow removing a lot of the code where you currently have to handle `DocSpan` objects. Instead each window will be completely self-contained after the transform.

As each window is just a list of integers, there is very little overhead in materializing the windows. Lists of integers are highly optimized in Python and we can optimizer more with numpy if needed. I think the introduction of DocSpan here is a sign of premature optimization.

> Do we really need to call this `spatial_size`? Is `size` not good enough? We use `size` absolutely everywhere.

That is the term we (before your time) decided to use. For example, the main helper function to extract the value from all our supported objects is

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/2d4484fba0f45637f68adadf5a056a6147642aa4/torchvision/transforms/v2/functional/_meta.py#L117

Our reason for this was twofold:

1. Calling it `image_size` excludes the use case of videos
2. Just calling it `size` can lead to ambiguities for bounding boxes, since for them the value is independent of the actual tensor shape. For all other datapoints `spatial_size` and `.shape` are tightly coupled.

Thus, I've opted to call it `spatial_size` here as well. I'm ok with changing it to a better name if we find one, if we reflect that in the actual API as well.

> decided to not address due to lack of time

That is not what I remember. We left it as is because all alternatives were way more complex. Just as a quick example: how would you call a dispatcher with a bounding box if the spatial size (or whatever we might call it) is not stored on the object itself?

> This is a bit of a conundrum. Do you know why some schedulers prefer batch and other epoch? Is it some derived property or more arbitrary?

Not for certain. Naturally if you wanted your lr to change smoothly you'd do it per batch rather than epoch. I'm just plugging in a scheduler as is written in its documentation.

> This is certainly not the way to go. Could we not just use whatever the default is?

A scheduler doesn't have a default. It has a step function which the user can use whenever and wherever they'd like. Setting mode `epoch` or `batch` as defaults will break backwards compatibility for anything that isn't intended to use the default mode we choose.

Since the Cyclic scheduler is a special case for which skorch hacked a solution, I suggested leaving that solution as is (so that it won't conflict with this feature or have compatibility issues) until skorch makes an update that breaks compatibility, and just get rid of that hack then.

For context, this is the kind of change that would require a major version bump. It's not a bug, in the sense that it's working as designed. (So now we discuss whether it's a good design)

Right now, one way to use the API is something like:
```py
if not is_address(random_input):
  raise ValidationError(f"{random_input} is not a valid address")
```

If we change it, it becomes something like:
```py
if not is_address(random_input):
  raise ValidationError(f"{random_input} is not a valid address")
elif is_checksum_formatted_address(random_input) and not is_checksum address(random_input):
  raise ValidationError(f"{random_input} is an invalid checksum")
```

It would be very easy to forget to do that second check. There are other cases where a typo in the address will cause it to return `False` for `is_address()`, like accidentally adding an extra character at the end, or dropping the leading 0 (but leaving the `x`). EOT
```bash
I will not get copied
```

Not quite, @ashb. "Essential container in task exited" is the stoppedReason when your task successfully finishes. 🌀

I _think_ you're probably right about some of the other stoppedReasons ("Container instance deregistration forced by user" jumps out), but the problem I'm trying to solve here is that when the host is terminated there is no other evidence of failure -- and I don't have any idea if those other stoppedReasons also have no evidence of failure or whether we can safely rely on the container's exit status. The container's exit status seems like the most precise indicator of success/failure, so I think it's preferable to use that indicator -- but in this edge case, that indicator fails.

So, you certainly may be correct that there are more edge cases, but I think we can limit this fix to the edge case we can prove.

I'm not sure `stacklevel` is necessary here, but if it is, the right answer is probably 2.

The problem is that because this is a decorator, the "natural" place for the warning to be emitted is inside the function it's wrapping, which is actually a level in the stack deeper than the one where the issue is actually raised.

Considering this warning is generated in response to a keyword argument to `setup` or a keyword in `setup.cfg`, the code that causes this is not present *anywhere* in the stack. Because the `requires` keyword can be specified in either location, I don't think there's a better place for this warning.

I think the wording of the warning itself is specific enough that hopefully people will understand what it means, but if not I don't think we can fix it with `stacklevel`.

```python
def log_print(batch_id, batch_num, metric, step_loss, log_interval, epoch_id=None, learning_rate=None):
    metric_nm, metric_val = metric.get()
    if not isinstance(metric_nm, list):
        metric_nm = [metric_nm]
        metric_val = [metric_val]

    # train log
    if (epoch_id is not None) and (learning_rate is not None):
        eval_str = '[Epoch %d Batch %d/%d] loss=%.4f, lr=%.7f, metrics: {' + \
                    ', '.join([i + ':%.4f' for i in metric_nm]) + '}'
        logging.info(eval_str, epoch_id + 1, batch_id + 1, batch_num, \
                             step_loss / log_interval, \
                             learning_rate, \
                             *metric_val)
    # inference log
    else:
        eval_str = '[Batch %d/%d] loss=%.4f, metrics: {' + \
                   ', '.join([i + ':%.4f' for i in metric_nm]) + '}'
        logging.info(eval_str, batch_id + 1, batch_num, \
                             step_loss / log_interval, \
                             *metric_val)
```

> Isn't that supposed to be relative to the directory containing package.json, not the current directory?

So I thought about this a bit when writing the test cases, and I believe the answer is "no", but I definitely want to hear your thoughts.

The most common use-case for putting `package.json` above pwd is a monorepo, where that `package.json` is serving multiple Pulumi projects. Suppose that `package.json` has a `main` field. That field will always point to the same entrypoint, no matter which Pulumi project is running. 

Instead, if `main` is relative to `pwd`, then you can universally rename your entrypoint. For example, suppose you wanted your entrypoint to be `main.ts` in every Pulumi project. But providing `"main": "main.ts"` in your `package.json`, you will override the default `index.js` for all Pulumi projects in the monorepo. I think that's the only time this would make sense.

----------

This is what you'd have if `main` is relative to `pwd`.
```
.
└── my-monorepo/
    ├── package.json
    ├── node_modoules/
    │   ├── @pulumi/pulumi
    │   └── ...
    ├── project-A/
    │   ├── Pulumi.yaml
    │   └── main.ts
    └── project-B/
        ├── Pulumi.yaml
        └── main.ts
```

This is what you'd have if main is relative to `package.json`
```
.
└── my-monorepo/
    ├── package.json
    ├── main.ts
    ├── node_modoules/
    │   ├── @pulumi/pulumi
    │   └── ...
    ├── project-A/
    │   └── Pulumi.yaml
    └── project-B/
        └── Pulumi.yaml
```

This precludes monorepos from using this feature meaningfully.

no, I don't think we need to change the data structure of Axis Mappings custom parameter. We only need to check that there are explicit mappings for the minimum, default and maximum values along each axis. Note that if we don't check and enforce that here, it will be varLib later on that will complain as it attempts to build the avar table.

The requirement that min, default and max be explicitly mapped does not mean there has to be necessarily three key:value pairs; because one of them (the default) may well be == to min or max. So the minimum number for a meaningful axis that does vary is two, with default == min or max. If there's only one mapping then the min == max == default and the variation axis is really just a point and there ought to be only one position for all the involved masters and instances for that axis.

Let's say that we have file with 6 lines (do not counting header), 3 rows and we want to read it with 2 partitions, so each partition should read 6/2=3 lines:
```
col1,col2,col3
"very very                      <---- this peace for partition №1
long string
that do not
-----------------------------
fit into partition",1,2     <---- and that peace for partition №2
3,4,5
6,7,8
```

https://github.com/dchigarev/modin/blob/8b5866337ce3e5e14b8c00c39e2ed465b4417a0e/modin/engines/base/io/text/text_file_reader.py#L30-L39

This change would belong in `ci.sh` to avoid duplication in `.travis.yml`. My plan is in the followup PR is to introduce a `-7` flag that tells `ci.sh` to use Python 3.7, similar to how we pass `-2` now. There, for `-7`, I will use `PY=python3.7`.

For now, we don't need to make this change to use `PY=`:

- Python 2 case: when we pass `-2`, we instruct `ci.sh` to use `./pants` to bootstrap the PEX and to not override interpreter constraints. `./pants` defaults to using Python 2.7 to bootstrap, so no changes there. Subprocesses will already resolve to 2.7, as it's the minimum acceptable interpreter discovered in CI.
- Python 3 case: we default in `ci.sh` to using `./pants3`, which will use the minimum acceptable Python 3 interpreter (3.6 in CI) to bootstrap the PEX. For subprocesses, we constrain to `CPython>3.6,CPython<4`, which resolves to 3.6 as it's the minimum acceptable interpreter.

Although not necessary, I can add to `ci.sh` that the bootstrap script when using Py3 should be `PY=python3.6 ./pants3` and the `PANTS_PYTHON_SETUP_INTERPRETER_CONSTRAINTS` should be `CPython==3.6.*`. I did change the message we print to say `Using Python 3.6`, so makes sense to change the code to make sure it truly is, rather than relying on implicit assumptions.

It is certainly useful to be able to define parameters in terms of numbers, or fractions. Quite often, one way of thinking is better for a particular problem.

Python allows us to define many different ways of providing parameters to a function. That said, I don't think the `units` of a named parameter should change based on an arbitrary threshold.

FYI: previously, if a fraction was provided, it would fail:
```
TypeError: 'float' object cannot be interpreted as an integer
```

this would also fail for `min_samples = 0.99999999`.

I also think of it in terms of optimization problems, where it might be useful to try and optimize the result, based on some kind of relaxed integer program. With this hybrid interpretation of min_samples, it requires the user to **undo** the guessing.

> basically now major versions will change

True

> and setting any upper limit will not have a strong effect

Not necessarily. It depends.
* Setting *any* irrelevant upper limit - or setting no upper limit - would risk our package break at cryptography X+3 version.
* Setting upper limit as `<X+3.0.0` would make sure our current package always work, because it will never install a potentially incompatible dependency in future. This approach implies that we the maintainers would need to keep an eye on upstream cryptography's major version release, and then test and/or review it, then bump our upper bound by 1. This would happen once every several months.

@pgokulak It might be possible to avoid rewriting existing code such as voigt_lineshape
For instance : 

```
lineshape = voigt_lineshape(...) # keep the default
lineshape *= self.add_chi_factor(self.input.molecule)  
```

Where most of the new code is added in  `add_chi_factor`:  

```
def add_chi_factor(molecule, w_centered, wv):
     """molecule, wavenumber array, voigt width"""
    if molecule == 'CO2':
        pressure_mbar = self.input.pressure_mbar
        mole_fraction = self.input.mole_fraction  # note, might be a dictionary if mulitple molecules ? check later
        Tgas = self.input.Tgas
        Tref = self.input.Tref

        xi_factor = get_xiFactor(pressure_mbar*1e-3, mole_fraction, Tgas, Tref)

     # xi_factor correction for pseudo-Lorentzian of Westlye et al.(2022)
        gamma_v = wv/2 # Voigt half-width
        alpha_xi = xi_factor*np.sin(np.pi/xi_factor)/(2*np.pi*gamma_v) # see see Westlye et al. (2022); Eqn(6)
        lineshape_correction = alpha_xi / (1 + abs(w_centered/gamma_v)**xi_factor) # see see Westlye et al. (2022); Eqn(5)

        return lineshape_correction
    else:
        raise NotImplementedError
```

`JournalDBCheckpoint = int` is just an alias usually used to get a more descriptive name to a type that would otherwise be cumbersome to use repetitively. From the perspective of the type system the alias is identical to the type it is aliased to.

On the other hand [`typing.NewType`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#newtype), really creates a different kind of type so that the type system can enforce it isn't mixed up with a different type somewhere else. In other words `JournalDBCheckpoint` would become entirely different from `int` and hence can `JournalDBCheckpoint(1) + 1` would be rejected from the type system.

I guess a [`typing.NewType`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#newtype) was meant to be used here.

> > What are the concern(s)?
> 
> > I guess, user will never need to worry or care about our additional `refs/west` space.
> 
> We're creating an unbounded amount of un-garbage-collectable refs here, including putting tags outside of refs/tags.
> 

Agree. And a very valid point indeed.
We should look into cleaning up this ref-space.
One option could be to simple wipe the `refs/west/` ref-space upon each `west update`.
That way we ensure remotely deleted branches are removed.
Removed tags are also removed, and so on.
The question is, how will this impact performance of fetching ?
And do we care, as the original reason for PR #188 was about being able to fetch the specific SHA, not about optimizing future fetches.

So if we keep performance out-of the loop, as this was never the intention, we can basically wipe whole `refs/west` as part of `west update`.
Or consider to just keep the refs currently pointed to be `manifest`, so that if manifest points to `my_branch` we keep that refs, as it can be useful for, eg. :
https://github.com/NordicPlayground/fw-nrfconnect-nrf/pull/1437
https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/west/issues/338 (have some rough ideas on using `refs/west` for checkpoints during a `west rebase`, that are wiped when rebase has completed).
Does that sound like an improvement ?

> Beyond not being able to collect the refs when they are no longer needed, it just feels weird.

Guess I can never take that feeling away from you :(

> and at least personally I'm bothered by all the `west/xyz` output I'm seeing showing up in `git log --oneline` output, for example. (E.g. all the backport branches if you have a downstream where zephyr isn't the manifest repository.)

Agree, valid point.
And cleaning out the parts of `refs/west` we don't actually need will for sure improve this.
Only reason I didn't do that in PR #188, was because I considered, `Why wipe it, if it improves performance of later fetches`.
So i'm fine with cleaning up the `refs/west` space, and then let `git gc` decide for how long potential SHAs are available.
It could mean additional fetches when switching between two very different manifest file, as example, switch between NCS and Zephyr as manifest project.
But I assume any degrade in fetch performance in this case is acceptable, as the performance will be similar to not having `refs/west` at all.

> 
> Within the Nordic downstream, I have always created a tag at the old point in history before rewriting history in any repository managed by west. This ensures an explicit pointer to the ref is available in a place where a user can expect to find it. 

And that is still true. We don't touch that tag, and it will be available until user removes it.
The tag is available where the user expects to find it.

> This feels like going behind the user's back in a weird way.

I disagree, we are not modifying anything the user has done.
But agree, current behavior would keep an additional ref to the same revision and that is what shows up in `git log`, and that seems to bother you, and I do acknowledge that, so will look into improving this.
And doing a little cleanup, as proposed above should help  :-)

You're using the old names for these. Our current preferred terms are:
- `universal config` for the contents of the cumulusci.yml that is part of CumulusCI itself.
- `global config` for the contents of ~/.cumulusci/cumulusci.yml (i.e. overrides that are specific to this user, but apply to all of their projects)
- `project config` for the contents of cumulusci.yml in the project repository
- `project local config` for the contents of ~/.cumulusci/[project name]/cumulusci.yml (i.e. overrides that are specific to this user and specific to one particular project)

This is the important thing:

```python
>>> ak.all(array[ak.argsort(array, axis=-1)] == ak.sort(array, axis=-1))
True
```

As for `axis=0`, the meaning of sorting with `axis=0` cuts across lists:

```python
>>> ak.sort(array, axis=0).tolist()
[[], [-7.0], [-5.0, 0.2], [-1.0, 3.3, 0.4], [0.3, 4.4, 3.0, -4.0], [0.6, 5.0, 5.5], [2.0, 6.0], [4.0], []]
```

That is, `-7.0` is the smallest number that is first in any list, `-5.0` is the second smallest number that is first in any list, and `0.2` is the smallest number that is second in any list. I don't know that it makes sense to maintain the shapes of all these lists when you're doing that, and I don't know what analysts would _want_ it to mean in the context of a data analysis.

At least, it's no mystery that `array[ak.argsort(array, axis=0)]` fails: the index positions returned by `ak.argsort` cut across all of the lists—they can be values that are much larger than the lengths of any of the lists.

```python
>>> ak.argsort(array, axis=0).tolist()
[[], [7], [5, 2], [1, 3, 4], [3, 4, 3, 4], [6, 5, 5], [2, 6], [4], []]
```

So what should we do about it? Maybe—without removing any of the the code that works—we should put in an exception that refuses to return results when `axis != -1` (or the equivalent positive `axis`, so this has to be in the C++ code, right after `posaxis` is determined from `wrap_axis_if_negative`). Then we're not promising that this is the behavior, such that someone can start relying on it before we know what the right policy should be. Also, if someone really does want `axis != -1 and axis is not None`, they'll complain that it's not working and we can ask them about their use-case and understand what the appropriate output would be.

What do you think?

Yes, it is because of the partial support of Azure Pipeline on Codecov.

In fact there is three level of supports for a CI:
1) flawless support: in this case, you do not need to provide `CODECOV_TOKEN`, and reports integrates flawlessly in Codecov
2) partial support: the reports integrates flawlessly in Codecov if you provide the token through `CODECOV_TOKEN`
3) no support: you can still push coverage reports if you provide the token through `CODECOV_TOKEN`, but because of missing optional other parameters consumed from the CI environment, reports are not integrated nicely

At this point, `python-coverage` is in situation 3), and `coverage-bash` is in situation 2).

That is why I moved to `coverage-bash`. I update the comment here.

Note: Out of band, we decided we use `assertions.PASS` instead of the behavior described in the comment. On which note, we should probably remove this comment.

> Isn't it possible that just other simple failures can cause issues?

We've already decided that any failed RPCs to the owner are going to be considered equivalent to the object being freed. Are you wanting to revisit that?

>Why doesn't the owner just sends a cancel subscription request to all subscribers?

That's not a resilient protocol, since the owner could have died, which would be equivalent to the object being freed. And subscription cancellation is only supposed to be down by the subscribers, and there's already logic to do that.

>Or can we use a specific status to decide if we'd like to cancel subscription? (e.g., instead of status.ok(), we can do something like !status.NotFound())

We don't have visibility into that status, [they all come back as `IOError` statuses](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/a0f73cf3f72d02b5f47f4a189052171a3fdd85c3/src/ray/common/grpc_util.h#L78-L89).

Here, we mix `int` type and `DType`. The second pointer may not be appropriately aligned with `DType`. You may consider to revise the code and add the `PadBytes`: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/1b02225fefd8ccc93bc73223f0d3cde103fad661/src/operator/tensor/ordering_op-inl.h#L350-L351

The reason to do this is that CUDA forces memory alignment, i.e., we will always have the following assertion:

```c++
DType* ptr;
ASSERT static_cast<size_t>(ptr) % sizeof(DType) == 0.
```

By default, calling cudaMalloc will give you a pointer with at least 256-bytes aligned address. However, after shifting the pointer to `ptr += ind.shape_.Size() * sizeof(int)`, the new address may not be aligned with `sizeof(DType)`.

```
for each out_x, out_y
    in_pts = InvM * out_pts
    output_image[out_pts] = interpolate(input_image, in_pts)
```
That's why now we need a method to get affine matrix and its inverse. We have a method to get inverse and now we need non-inverted one.

Pretty much:
1. Sample quantiles from partitions
  * Sample each dataframe
  * Pick 2*NPartitions.get() (henceforth N) quantiles from sample
  * Return that to head node
2. Compute 2N quantiles for the entire dataset from the 2N^2 quantiles on the head node
3. Broadcast 2N quantiles to each row partition, and use them to split each partition into 2N splits
4. Collect all N splits for each of the new 2N partitions, and join them together to result in 2N partitions
5. Lazily map a sort over all 2N partitions
6. Use our `rebalance_partitions` to ensure resulting partitions are similarly sized.

I’m curious to hear what reviewers think of this test pattern. My
thoughts:

  - I like that we’re testing the observable behavior of the real code
    with valid data providers, rather than relying on mocking or spying
    or anything brittle like that.
  - I considered using the existing multiplexer provider as sub-provider
    for these tests, but it felt inappropriate because the multiplexer
    provider doesn’t care about experiment IDs at all (not to mention
    that it touches disk).
  - Because the data provider interface is fairly large (many methods,
    many parameters), this test provider is fairly long, even though we
    skip tensors entirely.
  - In general, I like to introduce a kind of “configurable in-memory
    implementation” for things like this, so that in the test code you
    can say “add an experiment with this ID and this kind of data”. We
    don’t yet have one of those for the `DataProvider` interface itself.
    Would now be a good time to add one?

Overall, I think that these tests are reasonably robust, but I’m open to
ideas on how to improve them, along any axis.

> actually can this test just use local working dir?

Tasks can't take in [a local working dir](https://docs.ray.io/en/releases-1.12.1/ray-core/handling-dependencies.html#api-reference), so we would have to set the runtime_env in `ray.init()`. However, that runtime_env would also be shared by the Serve controller, so it would be able to deserialize this code, and the test would never fail.

> we really really need a local http version. (no one can push to your test_dag repo for example)

If I'm not mistaken, this always fails when `ssl_cert` consists of a leaf cert + intermediate CA(s). `openstack verify` only reads the first certificate of the chain in the file and ignores the rest.

If we take this openssl CLI approach, you need to split the content of `ssl_ca` into a leaf + the rest of the chain and do:
`openstack verify -CAfile "<ssl_ca>" -untrusted "<the rest of ssl_cert>" "<the first cert in ssl_cert"`.

`openstack verify -CAfile "<ssl_ca>" -untrusted "<ssl_cert>" "<ssl_cert>"` technically works to be simpler even though it's with a duplicate cert in the chain and it doesn't exactly match what Apache2 is doing.

The comment here that we return an empty array for not-found data is not
correct with the multiplexer provider:

```
$ curl localhost:6006/data/plugin/scalars/scalarsmulti -F tag=xent/xent_1 -F runs='["lr_1E-03,conv=1,fc=2"]'
{"lr_1E-03,conv=1,fc=2": [[1563406405.3048136, 2000, 0.01962822675704956]]}
$ curl localhost:6006/data/plugin/scalars/scalarsmulti -F tag=xent/xent_1 -F runs='["lr_1E-03,conv=1,fc=2","nope"]'
{"lr_1E-03,conv=1,fc=2": [[1563406405.3048136, 2000, 0.01962822675704956]]}
```

This is because the multiplexer provider follows the relevant portion of
the data provider contract for `read_scalars`:

> The result will only contain keys for run-tag combinations that
> actually exist, which may not include all entries in the
> `run_tag_filter`.

For what it’s worth, I think that this is the sensible behavior. It’s
fine not to fail the request, but returning an empty array when we have
the opportunity to just omit the key is just introducing error.

Not sure: last night I saw that `std::is_same<double, long double>::value` with MSVC is `false`, which is why I had to change my code to `sizeof(long double) == sizeof(double)` (b09e75b25c91bd300a1f2dbaabb1056a58d1d942), because that's what we really want to know.

> Not sure if this is worth handling.

I'm pretty sure no: https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/buffer.html isn't explicit about it (unfortunately), but it's strongly implied that endianness is assumed not to be mixed. Therefore I think going into that in the context of `buffer_info` will not help anything, on the contrary.

The situation is very different for serializing/deserializing arrays, then it has to be assumed endianness could be different between the producer and consumer, which could run on totally different platforms.

But the buffer interface is all about in-memory access. Hopefully we won't have one process (one address space) that mixes endianness. (It sounds like an idea from hell.)

I believe the confusion comes because `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CSL_RECEIVE_TIME_AHEAD` has two usages:

- When `!RadioSupportsReceiveTiming()`, is as you mention, and the constant is added to the "left" of the CSL sample time.
- When `RadioSupportsReceiveTiming()`, the constant is not taken into account for the duration of the receive slot, it's rather a safe margin used to schedule the delayed receive operation on time, taking into account serialization delays and so on.

What I'm fixing with this PR is the second case, for which currently OT code schedules the start of the receive window matching with the expected MHR time, so there is no margin for SHR + PHR.

This could be fixed with the usage of a constant (192 us), but there is room for energy efficiency improvements since it could be that the radio will get the frame even with partial reception of the preamble. So this allows to save a few microseconds of receive time on each CSL period.

> A single overload is wrong

I did this for two main reasons:

* I prefer the IDE behavior of a single overload, because jump-to-definition takes me to the implementation, rather than the `@overload` type signature. With `if TYPE_CHECKING:`, it will take me to the type-checking signature rather than the implementation, which I usually don't want (cough cough ConfigDict, 😩).

* I wanted it to be visually similar to the `@field_validator` decorator. In that case, because there are currently two signatures, the `@overload` is allowed. I'll note that even there, in principle we don't need two `@overload`s — with 
  ```python
  _V2ValidatorType = TypeVar(
      '_V2ValidatorType', bound=Union[V2WrapValidator, V2Validator, 'classmethod[Any]', 'staticmethod[Any]']
  )
  ```
  it could be expressed with one:
  ```python
  def field_validator(...) -> Callable[[_V2ValidatorType], _V2ValidatorType]:
  ```
  That's not to say that I'd suggest we change it — I prefer that it use @overload because it lets us have the different (compatible) signature in the implementation without resorting to the (in my opinion) less ergonomic `if TYPE_CHECKING:`.

-----

At any rate, I am happy using any of the following approaches:

* Single `@overload` (current approach)
* Changing the single `@overload` to `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
* Just dropping the `@overload` signature (and losing the mypy error for not specifying even one field name)
* Changing the implementation signature to `(__field, *fields, ...` and relying on the missing-positional-argument TypeError produced by python, rather than the more explicit error message.

Just let me know which you prefer.

What Abtin wrote about managing TCP buffers is correct.

Regarding `otMessage`, the TCP stack does not use them because it could lead to deadlock under low-memory situations (e.g,. TCP cannot allocate a packet to send out data because too many `otMessage`s are tied up in its send queue).

I recognize that the `otLinkedBuffer`s take more effort to use. To help alleviate this, we provide a circular buffer library (see `tcp_ext.h`) that does much of that management automatically, and provides the user of TCP with a fixed-size buffer abstraction for the send buffer. A "monkeypatch" would need to somehow intercept the `go list` invocation invoked by `go tool cover`. Note in the [source code though](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/a0441c7ae3dea57a0553c9ea77e184c34b7da40f/src/cmd/cover/func.go#L202), `go tool cover` specifically uses its own `GOROOT` to find and invoke `go list`.  Trying to intercept `go tool cover` in this manner is extremely brittle if it can be accomplished as it is.

As for delegating to html.go, that would involve cloning the upstream `go` repo somehow. The relevant functions are not exported from its package and so they cannot be consumed as a third-party library.

In any event, this begs the question: Should Pants provide the _same_ experience or an _equivalent_ experience? Here, we should provide _equivalent_ even if it could diverge in the future.

The HTML report generated by this PR matches existing `go tool cover` behavior without any brittle trickery to obtain the _same_ experience. If that experience diverges in the future, the report generated by this PR is still useful and valid, regardless of what the upstream does.

Hey @iromeo, sorry, I haven't had time to formulate a proposal how I think this should be approached, but here a couple of thoughts:
* IMO, the same model/device family (like `shuii.humidifier.jsq*` in this case) should be implemented using a simple class that adapts to the model-specific differences. This means sharing the same data classes (for status) and raising exceptions when unavailable methods are called. 
* Different modes: this library really needs a common interface for the features (or settings) that apply to different devices. A first step could be something like adding `supported_modes` (or whatever naming fits the best) that returns a `dict[str, int]` and `set_mode_by_name(str)`. This would be similar to what `roborock.Vacuum` has with `fanspeed_presets` to inform homeassistant for the available fan speeds.
* For generic functionality, enums (or booleans where applicable) should be preferred, as those can be type-checked statically. Maybe `set_led(bool)` as a generic interface suffices until we find a proper solution to provide different "settings" in a common way? The implementations can keep having their separate, internal implementations, but it'd be great to have a stable interface to avoid imports like this https://github.com/syssi/xiaomi_airpurifier/blob/develop/custom_components/xiaomi_miio_airpurifier/fan.py#L31-L74 .

I'll see if I can find some time to implement a common interface for vacuums based using `typing.Protocol` that could be used as an inspiration for similar interface for humidifers, but I cannot promise currently anything.

tldr; my concern is that as there are currently no strict API contracts, it is awfully complicated to use this lib, e.g., in homeassistant as it is necessary to adapt parts of the code depending on the device and the implementation.

To briefly answer your explicit questions:

> Should I replace enums with str values?

Enums are better (as they can be consolidated far easier in the future) than strings.

> Is it better to use for Status mode device raw value (int or str) or better report some string presentable names? Maybe for dependent projects string names are better. Because users will use them in their automation, etc. code when comparing device state with some desired state.

Raw, device-reported values should not be used as-is as due to the reasons above.

> If do not use enums, should I extend AirHumidifierStatus and override signatures of def mode(self) -> OperationMode: and other properties? Or better to have an independent status class?

As long as the class is exchangeable with the existing one, it's fine. I would rather see the current class extended to cover new devices.

> I could modify *jsq001 API also (I was a contributor). This will break compatibility with https://github.com/syssi/xiaomi_airpurifier project, but there I'm working on PR for *jsq002 support, so I could update integrations with both devices. If we use presentable names for status mode values, user automations shouldn't be broken. * jsq001 isn't supported by HA, so not changes in HA required. The functions in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/b6c71e5e941b885b1a5169d460c430df9d392fa9/R-package/src/lightgbm_R.h has a return type `LGBM_SE`, which is the LightGBM equivalent of R's built-in `SEXP` type.

If you change those routines to all be `void` (since they modify their inputs by reference and don't technically have to refer to anything), errors are thrown about not being able to convert a null to `SEXP`.

So the `call_state` thing is a simple way to allocate an `LGBM_SE` (an R integer, in this case) that can then be returned.

As part of #3016 we'll be able to remove these unnecessary `call_state` things, but I believe they're harmless for now.

Since the iterating is done lazily in `NeighborQueryResult`, we cannot index into the `NeighborList` to get all 5 pieces of information like we can with the `NeighborList`. If we decide we want to keep it symmetric between `NeighborList` and `NeighborQueryResult`, that would mean that iterating over a `NeighborList` should return all 5 pieces of information also.

In general, I don't think users find the `NeighborQueryResult` to be a very useful object in python (I've had people in lab ask me why it even exists in the first place), so I don't have a huge opinion on whether they should match or not. It also takes very little time to generate the full `NeighborList` from the `NeighborQueryResult`, so after thinking it over again, I'm okay leaving everything as is for now.

We're forcing all transforms to accept a `flow` and a `valid` parameter, even if those aren't used. Typically for Sintel where `valid` is always None, `valid` would always be a passthough. An example of such transform would be e.g.

```py
class RandomHorizontalFlip(T.RandomHorizontalFlip):
    def forward(self, img1, img2, flow, valid):
        if torch.rand(1) > self.p:
            return img1, img2, flow, valid

        img1 = F.hflip(img1)
        img2 = F.hflip(img2)
        flow = F.hflip(flow) * torch.tensor([-1, 1])[:, None, None]
        if valid is not None:
            valid = F.hflip(valid)
        return img1, img2, flow, valid
```

Agree with @funbaker 

As to the capabilities and how they are matched up with user credentials, before this PR, the securitymethods were parsed but not used (other than printing them out in describe) as far as I know.  This is the first usage of these classes and parts of the capabilities.

The new code in the PR has the ability to look at the capabilities of the service, and what creds it has been given by the user, and matching that up to see if anything works.  [0] is a description of the steps there.

The steps in [0] match up the methods against what creds we have for that authsession.  But past the method (cookie, basic, cert), it doesn't give any hint as to which creds of that type it is looking for.  For example, it may say tls-with-certificate and pass a client certificate, but it doesn't say that the certificate needs to come from a particular authority or have a special field.

Let's go through a somewhat complex example that points out some of the trickiness.  Say you have two certificates now, one for Gaia, and one for CADC.  And you want to use the Gaia cert to auth with Gaia, and CADC cert to auth with CADC.  From the capabilities of each service, they could both say tls-with-certificate.  But they don't say anything about how to pick which cert they are talking about (there's no subject line, or signing authority, etc).

If you pick the wrong cert, you are basically disclosing it to the wrong service, which is exposing your creds (hopefully they don't log it :smile: ).  Similar cases exist for username/password.

The underlying calling pattern is due to the ambiguous nature of which creds go with what services, and for security, it's dangerous to guess. Without the knowledge to figure out which creds go with what service from the capabilities, we have to rely on the user to tell us by which creds should be used for what services.  

We can add in more helpers to set this up in an easier way, but I was also trying to make this a change that people could use, but wouldn't possibly break anyone that wasn't using it.  I guess some of this gets into how "first-class" do you want an extension to be in the API?

[0]
1) Make an authsession and attach your creds to it by adding providers.  This is done by putting in the URI of the security method and the requests class to handle that URI. 
2) Attach the authsession to a service, it gets the capabilities of the service using pyvo and requests to get the XML document, and it parses out the security methods.
3) That is passed into the authurls class, which iterates over the interfaces in the capabilities, and logs their URLs, base or full, and their security method URI.
4) Now the user makes a request.  The session gets the URL of the request, and tries to match it up against URLs in the interfaces mentioned in step 2, taking into account full and base paths.  It comes up with a list of the auth methods that the server will accept.
5) The intersection of the server methods is taken with the creds that have been put in the jar.  Then we pick one and use it for auth for that particular request.

@librasungirl what is the background of this change?

I think the existing code is correct and this change will make extra long wait time between first announce send and the next one on the next channel.
- Note that we stay in `kAttachStateAnnounce` state for multiple iterations (of `AttachTimer` firing), each time sending announce on a different channel.
- Each iteration uses `mAnnounceDelay` wait before sending the next announce (on the next channel).
- `mAnnounceDelay` is calculated as `kAnnounceTimeout / (channelMask.GetNumberOfChannels() + 1)`
   - We also have a check where if the calculated `mAnnounceDelay` is too short (less than `kMinAnnounceDelay`) we then use the min value (which `80` msec) between two announce txs on different channels.
- This means overall we wait at least for `kAnnounceTimeout` which is `1400` msec and is longer than `kParentRequestReedTimeout` (which is `1250` msec).
